topics.title: Storage Engines Are Architectural Decisions
topics.summaryShort: Storage engines define system behavior under load more than APIs do. Latency profiles, failure modes, and scaling characteristics are direct consequences of engine design choices. Treat them as first-class architecture decisions, not implementation details.
keyPoints: Engine design dictates read/write amplification and tail latency behavior
Operational characteristics like compaction, vacuuming, and fragmentation shape SLOs
Workload shape must align with engine strengths, not the other way around
articleSections: At scale, the database is not a black box; it is a deterministic machine with predictable failure modes. Whether your engine is append-only, LSM-based, or page-oriented directly affects tail latency and throughput stability. For example, compaction-heavy systems can exhibit latency spikes under sustained writes. Conversely, page-based systems suffer from fragmentation and random I/O penalties under write-heavy workloads. These are not edge cases; they are steady-state behaviors at scale.

Read amplification and write amplification are the core levers. Systems that optimize writes, such as LSM trees, push cost into reads and background compaction. Systems that optimize reads, such as B-trees, pay with expensive random writes and page splits. There is no free lunch; you are choosing where the system burns cycles. Principal-level design requires explicitly stating which amplification you are willing to absorb.

Operationally, storage engines introduce continuous work: compaction, rebalancing, cache warming, and recovery. These background tasks compete with foreground traffic and can destabilize latency if not controlled. The more your workload deviates from the engine's assumptions, the more operational tuning you inherit. This becomes a reliability problem, not just a performance one.

In interviews and architecture reviews, elevate the discussion from which database to which storage behavior under this workload. Frame decisions in terms of access patterns, write rates, durability requirements, and acceptable tail latency. This reframing signals maturity and prevents cargo-cult technology choices.
topics.example: A high-throughput event ingestion system chooses an LSM-based store for sustained write rates, accepting higher read amplification and adding a caching layer to protect read latency.
topics.interviewTakeaway: Always map workload characteristics to storage engine behavior and explicitly choose where amplification costs land.

topics.title: Append-Only Logs + Hash Indexes: Fast Writes, Fragile Reads
topics.summaryShort: Append-only logs paired with in-memory hash indexes maximize write throughput with minimal coordination. The tradeoff is poor range query support and reliance on memory for fast lookups. Compaction becomes the hidden cost center.
keyPoints: Sequential writes minimize disk seek and maximize throughput
Hash indexes require memory residency for performance
Compaction is mandatory to reclaim space and control read cost
articleSections: The append-only log model treats storage as a write-optimized stream. Every update is an append, eliminating in-place mutations and avoiding random I/O. A hash index maps keys to log offsets, enabling O(1) lookups if the index fits in memory. This design shines under high write throughput with simple key-value access patterns. It is fundamentally aligned with modern SSD characteristics and log-structured thinking.

However, this design collapses when access patterns deviate. Range queries are inefficient because data is not ordered by key. If the hash index cannot fit in memory, lookup performance degrades sharply due to additional disk seeks. Additionally, deleted or overwritten values accumulate, inflating storage and degrading scan performance. The system becomes increasingly dependent on compaction.

Compaction rewrites the log, removing obsolete entries and merging segments. This process is I/O intensive and competes with foreground traffic. Poorly tuned compaction leads to latency spikes and unpredictable throughput. At scale, compaction scheduling becomes as critical as request handling.

This model is ideal for write-heavy, point-lookup workloads with bounded key cardinality. It is a poor fit for analytics, range scans, or memory-constrained environments. The design conversation should explicitly include index residency assumptions and compaction budgets.
topics.example: A session store for millions of active users uses an append-only log with an in-memory hash index to support rapid updates and expirations, periodically compacting to remove stale sessions.
topics.interviewTakeaway: Use append-only plus hash indexes for write-heavy point lookups, but plan explicitly for memory pressure and compaction overhead.

topics.title: SSTables and LSM Trees: Trading Writes for Compaction Debt
topics.summaryShort: LSM trees turn random writes into sequential writes using immutable SSTables. They excel at write throughput but accumulate compaction debt and read amplification. The system's real complexity lies in managing these tradeoffs over time.
keyPoints: Writes are batched and flushed sequentially, minimizing random I/O
Reads may touch multiple SSTables, increasing read amplification
Compaction strategy determines long-term performance stability
articleSections: LSM trees buffer writes in memory and flush them as sorted, immutable SSTables. This transforms random writes into sequential disk operations, dramatically improving write throughput. The immutability simplifies concurrency and crash recovery. However, it introduces fragmentation across multiple SSTables that must be reconciled during reads.

Read amplification emerges because a single key may exist across multiple SSTables. Bloom filters mitigate unnecessary disk reads, but they do not eliminate the need to check multiple levels. As data grows, the number of SSTables increases, and so does the cost of reads. This is a deliberate tradeoff in favor of write efficiency.

Compaction is the core maintenance mechanism, merging SSTables and discarding obsolete entries. Different compaction strategies, such as leveled versus size-tiered, shift the balance between write amplification and read latency. Aggressive compaction improves reads but increases write amplification and I/O pressure. Lazy compaction reduces write cost but degrades read performance.

LSM systems are powerful but operationally complex. You must budget I/O for compaction, tune memory for buffering, and design around eventual consistency in reads. In interviews, emphasize how you control compaction debt and protect tail latency under sustained write load.
topics.example: A time-series ingestion system uses an LSM-based database to handle millions of writes per second, tuning leveled compaction to keep read latency predictable for recent-data queries.
topics.interviewTakeaway: LSM trees buy write throughput at the cost of compaction and read amplification, so design how you manage that debt.

topics.title: B-Trees and In-Place Updates: Read Efficiency with Write Penalties
topics.summaryShort: B-trees optimize for predictable reads and range queries through ordered, page-based storage. The cost is random I/O and write amplification from page splits and in-place updates. Fragmentation and caching become dominant concerns at scale.
keyPoints: Ordered structure enables efficient range queries and stable read latency
In-place updates cause random I/O and page splits
Caching and fragmentation management are critical for performance
articleSections: B-trees organize data into fixed-size pages with keys kept in sorted order. This structure enables efficient range scans and consistent read performance, making it ideal for read-heavy workloads. The tree height remains shallow, keeping lookup depth predictable. However, this design assumes that random I/O is acceptable or mitigated by caching.

Writes are more expensive because they involve modifying pages in place. Page splits and rebalancing introduce additional I/O and can cascade up the tree. On SSDs, this translates into write amplification at the storage layer as well. Under heavy write workloads, this becomes a limiting factor.

Fragmentation accumulates over time, especially with frequent updates and deletes. Vacuuming or reorganization is required to reclaim space and maintain performance. Meanwhile, buffer pool tuning is essential to keep hot pages in memory and avoid disk reads. Misconfigured caching leads to dramatic latency degradation.

B-trees are the right choice when read predictability and range queries dominate. They are less suitable for high-ingest workloads without batching or write buffering. In interviews, articulate how you handle write pressure and maintain cache efficiency.
topics.example: An e-commerce catalog service uses a B-tree-based database to support complex filtering and range queries on product attributes with predictable read latency.
topics.interviewTakeaway: Choose B-trees for read stability and range queries, but account for write amplification and fragmentation.

topics.title: Secondary Indexes: The Hidden Write Tax
topics.summaryShort: Secondary indexes improve query flexibility but multiply write costs and operational complexity. Every write becomes multiple writes, often across different data structures. Index maintenance is a primary driver of latency under write-heavy workloads.
keyPoints: Each secondary index introduces additional write amplification
Consistency between primary and secondary indexes adds coordination cost
Index design must reflect real query patterns, not hypothetical ones
articleSections: Secondary indexes are essentially additional data structures that must be kept in sync with the primary data. Every insert, update, or delete propagates to all relevant indexes. This multiplies write amplification and increases latency variability. The cost grows linearly with the number of indexes.

Maintaining consistency between indexes introduces coordination overhead. In distributed systems, this may involve multi-phase writes or eventual consistency models. Failures can leave indexes temporarily inconsistent, requiring repair mechanisms. This adds both complexity and operational risk.

From a storage perspective, secondary indexes often duplicate large portions of data. This increases storage footprint and affects cache efficiency. Reads may improve for certain queries but at the expense of overall system throughput. Poorly chosen indexes can degrade performance rather than improve it.

The key is to design indexes based on actual query patterns and SLAs. Avoid speculative indexing and continuously evaluate index utility. In interviews, demonstrate awareness of index maintenance costs and how you limit unnecessary write amplification.
topics.example: A social feed service adds a secondary index on user_id for fast timeline queries, but later removes rarely used indexes after observing increased write latency during peak traffic.
topics.interviewTakeaway: Every index is a write tax; add only what your workload justifies and revisit continuously.

topics.title: In-Memory Databases: Latency Wins, Durability Tradeoffs
topics.summaryShort: In-memory databases eliminate disk I/O from the critical path, delivering predictable low latency. The tradeoff is durability complexity and memory cost constraints. Persistence strategies define their real-world reliability.
keyPoints: Memory-first design minimizes latency and avoids disk contention
Durability relies on snapshots, logs, or replication strategies
Memory cost and dataset size limit applicability
articleSections: In-memory databases store the working dataset entirely in RAM, removing disk I/O from the request path. This enables microsecond-level latency and highly predictable performance. Data structures can be optimized for CPU cache locality rather than disk layout. This fundamentally changes the performance envelope.

Durability is achieved through mechanisms like append-only logs, periodic snapshots, or replication. Each approach introduces tradeoffs in recovery time and data loss windows. For example, snapshotting reduces recovery complexity but increases potential data loss. Append-only logging improves durability but adds write overhead.

Memory constraints limit dataset size and increase cost. Systems must either shard aggressively or accept bounded datasets. Eviction policies become critical when memory pressure increases. Poor eviction strategies can destabilize performance and correctness.

In-memory systems are ideal for latency-sensitive workloads such as caching, real-time analytics, and session management. They are less suitable for large, durable datasets without hybrid designs. In interviews, focus on how you balance latency, durability, and cost.
topics.example: A real-time bidding system uses an in-memory store for bidder state to ensure sub-millisecond decision latency, persisting asynchronously to a durable store for recovery.
topics.interviewTakeaway: Use in-memory databases when latency is paramount, but explicitly design for durability and memory limits.

topics.title: Clustered vs Secondary Indexes: Locality Is a Query Contract
topics.summaryShort: Index design is not just about lookup speed; it defines how data is physically co-located and therefore how expensive real queries become. Clustered layouts reward locality-heavy access patterns, while secondary indexes buy flexibility at the cost of additional pointer chasing and storage duplication. Principal engineers think about indexes as physical query contracts, not logical conveniences.
keyPoints: Clustered storage aligns record layout with the dominant access path and improves locality-sensitive reads.
Secondary indexes improve query flexibility but often degrade locality and increase read indirection.
Index choice should follow the dominant query path and SLO, not abstract normalization preferences.
articleSections: A clustered index determines the physical order of records on disk, which means it can drastically change how many pages a common query must touch. If the dominant access pattern follows that order, the engine benefits from prefetch, sequential reads, and better cache locality. This is why clustered storage matters disproportionately for range scans, time-ordered retrieval, and workloads that repeatedly walk adjacent keys. The physical layout becomes part of the query plan rather than an invisible implementation detail.

Secondary indexes, by contrast, usually point to rows stored elsewhere. That indirection is acceptable when the matching set is small, but it becomes expensive when a query fan-outs into large row fetches. The engine may first scan the secondary structure and then bounce across the heap or primary table to retrieve the rest of the record, multiplying cache misses and random I/O. This is where a logically good index can still produce poor real-world latency.

The deeper design insight is that locality and flexibility compete. A system optimized for many ad hoc predicates often accumulates multiple secondary indexes, while a system optimized for a few dominant access paths may benefit more from clustered or covering layouts that make those paths extremely cheap. The correct choice depends on whether the workload is exploratory, operational, or latency-critical. Treating every index as morally equivalent hides these physical consequences.

In architecture reviews, do not stop at saying we will add an index on that field. Ask whether the workload needs point lookups, ordered retrieval, or full-record locality after the match. That is the level where you can distinguish between a helpful index and an index that merely shifts cost into another part of the storage path.
topics.example: A metrics service clusters data by tenant and timestamp so common recent-range reads stay sequential, while secondary indexes on alert dimensions are used sparingly because wide matches would otherwise fan out into expensive heap lookups.
topics.interviewTakeaway: When discussing indexes, explain not only what they filter on but also whether they preserve locality or force costly secondary lookups.

topics.title: OLTP vs Analytics: One Storage Shape Rarely Serves Both
topics.summaryShort: Operational databases and analytical systems optimize for different truths. OLTP favors point lookups, write isolation, and small transactions, while analytics favors large scans, aggregation efficiency, and columnar compression. Treating them as the same workload leads to architectural compromise and predictable pain.
keyPoints: OLTP and analytical workloads stress storage engines in fundamentally different ways.
Row-oriented layouts favor transactional access, while columnar layouts favor scan-heavy aggregation.
A principal design should separate system-of-record concerns from large-scale analytical read paths when the workload justifies it.
articleSections: Transactional systems are optimized for small, selective queries that mutate or retrieve a few records with strong correctness guarantees. Their storage engines favor row-oriented layouts, index-assisted lookups, and concurrency control that protects short-lived business operations. Analytical workloads want the opposite: broad scans, large aggregations, and efficient access to only a few columns across many rows. For that style of access, row stores waste I/O and cache by repeatedly loading columns that the query does not need.

Columnar systems reverse those tradeoffs. By storing each column together, they compress better, vectorize better, and scan far less irrelevant data for aggregates or reporting queries. The downside is that point writes and row reconstruction become more expensive, which is why column stores are rarely ideal as primary transactional databases. This is not a matter of one being newer or smarter; they are optimized for different physical access patterns.

Many production incidents come from pretending one engine can satisfy both worlds indefinitely. Heavy analytical queries run against the operational store, saturate caches, evict hot transactional pages, and destabilize end-user latency. The system then acquires read replicas, ETL pipelines, or a warehouse not because the team suddenly discovered analytics, but because the workload mismatch finally became too expensive to ignore.

In interviews, the mature framing is to separate the write-authoritative transactional path from the large-scan analytical path when volumes and query shapes diverge. You should explain when that split becomes necessary, how data is transferred, and what freshness guarantees the analytical side actually needs. That shows an understanding of workload-specific storage design rather than generic database shopping.
topics.example: A SaaS billing platform keeps subscriptions and invoices in an OLTP PostgreSQL cluster for transactional correctness, then streams changes into a columnar warehouse so finance dashboards and cohort analysis do not interfere with payment-path latency.
topics.interviewTakeaway: If a system has both transactional and analytical access patterns, say explicitly when you would separate them and why one storage layout should not absorb both forever.
